pain_point_label: Blind Spots on Competitive & Market Dynamics

matches.match_rationale: This pain point is almost a textbook use case for a research agent: the operator lacks competitive intelligence on rival schools and demographic data about their catchment areas, problems that require ongoing, multi-source information gathering rather than a one-time analysis. A research agent can autonomously scrape competitor fee structures, enrollment announcements, new campus openings, and government demographic releases, synthesizing them into a periodic briefing. This is exactly the autonomous multi-source gather-and-synthesize pattern the capability is defined around.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An agent that runs weekly, pulling data from competitor websites, government census releases, and local news, then delivers a structured competitive landscape briefing to enrollment leadership — flagging new entrants, fee changes, demographic shifts, and catchment population trends.

matches.match_rationale: Multi-site operators are flooded with potentially relevant long-form inputs — government reports, competitor annual reports, local council planning documents, and industry whitepapers — but have no capacity to read them systematically. Summarization directly addresses the mechanism: turning voluminous external documents into digestible strategic intelligence. The output is a denser, preserved-meaning version of inputs that leadership can act on without reading everything themselves.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An automated pipeline that ingests newly published government demographic reports, competitor prospectuses, and regional planning documents, summarizing each into a one-page brief tagged by strategic theme (pricing, catchment, new campuses).

matches.match_rationale: Demographic shifts in catchment areas are inherently time-series problems: birth rate cohorts, net migration flows, and population growth all produce time-ordered signals that predict future enrollment-eligible populations. An operator currently blind to these shifts can use time-series forecasting on public ABS/census data combined with their own historical enrollment figures to project demand per campus 2-5 years out — directly informing strategic decisions about capacity and positioning.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A forecasting model ingesting historical enrollment by campus, local birth registry data, and population projections to produce campus-level demand forecasts, surfacing which sites face enrolment risk from demographic decline and which have untapped growth.

matches.match_rationale: Competitive and market intelligence lives in unstructured text — news articles, competitor websites, government statements, industry reports — and the relationships between entities (school X opened campus Y in suburb Z, operator A reduced fees after competitor B, demographic trend C is affecting catchment D) are exactly the structured relational facts that knowledge extraction is designed to surface. This goes beyond summarization: it produces a queryable knowledge graph of market dynamics rather than just condensed prose.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A knowledge extraction pipeline that continuously processes competitor web pages, local news, and public filings to build and update a structured competitive map — tracking which schools are opening, closing, raising fees, or targeting new demographics — queryable by enrollment strategists.
